message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic has highlighted an urgent need to use infection testing databases to rapidly estimate effectiveness of prior infection in preventing reinfection (
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) by novel variants of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Mathematical modeling was used to demonstrate the applicability of the test-negative, case-control study design to derive
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . Modeling was also used to investigate effects of bias in
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    estimation. The test-negative design was applied to national-level testing data in Qatar to estimate
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    for SARS-CoV-2 infection and to validate this design.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Apart from the very early phase of an epidemic, the difference between the test-negative estimate for
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and the true value of
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    was minimal and became negligible as the epidemic progressed. The test-negative design provided robust estimation of
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    even when
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    began to wane after prior infection. Assuming that only 25% of prior infections are documented, misclassification of prior infection status underestimated
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    , but the underestimate was considerable only when >50% of the population was ever infected. Misclassification of latent infection, misclassification of current active infection, and scale-up of vaccination all resulted in negligible bias in estimated
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>PE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    against SARS-CoV-2 Alpha and Beta variants was estimated at 97.0% (95% CI: 93.6-98.6) and 85.5% (95% CI: 82.4-88.1), respectively. These estimates were validated using a cohort study design.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The test-negative design offers a feasible, robust method to estimate protection from prior infection in preventing reinfection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Estimating protection afforded by prior infection in preventing reinfection: Applying the test-negative study design
